categories.title: Preparing for Surgery
categories.introText: Learn what to expect before your heart surgery, including pre-operative tests, fasting guidelines, and how to prepare your home for recovery.

categories.title: The Procedure
categories.introText: Understand the step-by-step process of open-heart surgery, anesthesia, and what happens in the operating room.

categories.title: Post-Op Care
categories.introText: Detailed guidance on wound care, activity restrictions, follow-up appointments, and what signs to watch for after surgery.

categories.title: Medication Guide
categories.introText: A comprehensive review of the medications prescribed after surgery, their purposes, dosing schedules, and possible side effects.

categories.title: Lifestyle & Diet
categories.introText: Heart-healthy nutrition, physical activity recommendations, smoking cessation, and stress management for long-term recovery.

categories.title: Warning Signs
categories.introText: Know when to call your care team or go to the emergency room. Recognize symptoms that require immediate attention.

topics.title: Initial Recovery: Day 1 & 2
topics.body:
After your heart surgery, the first 24 to 48 hours are focused on stabilization. You will likely wake up in the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) where medical staff will monitor your vitals closely.

### Breathing Exercises
It is crucial to use your incentive spirometer every hour while awake. This helps prevent lung complications such as pneumonia. Take a deep breath in through the device, hold for 3 seconds, and exhale slowly.

### Early Mobility
The nursing team will assist you in sitting on the edge of the bed or moving to a chair. Movement is essential for circulation and digestive health. Always use your heart pillow to "splint" your chest when moving or coughing.

> "Recovery is a marathon, not a sprint. Take small steps each day and communicate any discomfort with your nurse immediately."

Your pain will be managed through a combination of medications. Inform your care team if your pain level exceeds a 4 out of 10.

topics.title: Fasting Guidelines
topics.body:
To reduce the risk of aspiration during anesthesia, you must follow strict fasting guidelines before your surgery. Typically, you should stop eating solid foods at midnight the night before your procedure.

You may drink clear liquids (water, apple juice, black coffee) up to four hours before your scheduled arrival time. However, your surgical team may provide specific instructions that differ — always follow the guidelines given to you directly by your care team.

topics.title: Preparing Your Home
topics.body:
Creating a comfortable and safe environment at home before surgery will greatly ease your recovery. Set up a rest area on the main floor to avoid stair climbing in the first week. Stock up on easy-to-prepare, heart-healthy foods. Arrange for a trusted person to stay with you for the first 1–2 weeks.

Remove trip hazards such as loose rugs. Place frequently used items at waist height to avoid bending. Have a shower chair or non-slip mat ready. Prepare loose, comfortable clothing with front closures to make dressing easier.

topics.title: Anesthesia Explained
topics.body:
You will receive general anesthesia for your heart surgery, meaning you will be completely unconscious and pain-free throughout the procedure. An anesthesiologist — a specialist physician — will monitor your vitals continuously and adjust medication as needed.

Before going to sleep, you will be given a sedative through an IV. Once asleep, a breathing tube will be placed to support your lungs, and a urinary catheter will be inserted. The anesthesia team will remain by your side for the entire duration of your surgery.

topics.title: The Heart-Lung Machine
topics.body:
In most open-heart surgeries, a heart-lung bypass machine (cardiopulmonary bypass) temporarily takes over the work of your heart and lungs. This machine oxygenates your blood and pumps it through your body, allowing the surgeon to operate on a still, bloodless heart.

A perfusionist operates the machine throughout the procedure, carefully managing blood flow, temperature, and oxygenation. Off-pump surgery is available for some patients and avoids the use of the bypass machine entirely.

topics.title: Surgical Incisions
topics.body:
Traditional open-heart surgery involves a median sternotomy — an incision through the center of the chest and the breastbone (sternum). This provides the surgeon with direct access to the heart.

Minimally invasive approaches may use smaller incisions between the ribs for certain procedures. These techniques may reduce recovery time and risk of infection. Your surgeon will determine the most appropriate approach based on your specific anatomy and the complexity of the procedure.

topics.title: Wound Care
topics.body:
Keeping your surgical incision clean and dry is essential to prevent infection. You will receive specific instructions from your surgical team, but generally: gently clean the incision with mild soap and water daily; pat thoroughly dry; do not apply lotions, creams, or ointments unless directed.

Monitor your incision daily for signs of infection: increasing redness, swelling, warmth, drainage, or fever above 38°C (100.4°F). If any of these occur, contact your healthcare provider immediately. Wear loose clothing to avoid rubbing on the incision.

topics.title: Activity Restrictions
topics.body:
Your sternum (breastbone) was cut during surgery and needs approximately 6–8 weeks to heal. During this period, avoid lifting anything heavier than 4.5 kg (10 lbs), pushing or pulling heavy objects, and reaching both arms overhead simultaneously.

Walking is encouraged from day one — start with short walks and gradually increase distance each day. Avoid driving until cleared by your surgeon, typically 4–6 weeks post-operatively or until you are off all opioid pain medication. Sexual activity can usually resume 4–6 weeks after surgery when you are comfortable.

topics.title: Follow-Up Appointments
topics.body:
Attending all scheduled follow-up appointments is critical to a safe recovery. Your first appointment is typically 1–2 weeks after discharge for wound check and staple or suture removal. A cardiology visit is usually scheduled at 4–6 weeks and again at 3 months.

Bring a list of all your current medications to every appointment. Your care team will monitor your heart rhythm, blood pressure, and healing progress. Cardiac rehabilitation — a medically supervised exercise and education program — is strongly recommended and referrals are typically made at the 4–6 week visit.

topics.title: Blood Thinners
topics.body:
Many patients require anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications after heart surgery to prevent blood clots. Common medications include warfarin (Coumadin), aspirin, clopidogrel (Plavix), and rivaroxaban (Xarelto).

If you are prescribed warfarin, you will need regular blood tests (INR monitoring) to ensure your dose is correct. Keep all lab appointments and report any unusual bleeding or bruising to your care team immediately. Avoid NSAIDs (such as ibuprofen or naproxen) unless approved by your doctor, as they can increase bleeding risk.

topics.title: Beta-Blockers
topics.body:
Beta-blockers are commonly prescribed to control heart rate and blood pressure after cardiac surgery. They work by blocking the effects of adrenaline on the heart, reducing workload and the risk of arrhythmias.

Common examples include metoprolol (Lopressor), carvedilol (Coreg), and bisoprolol. Take these medications exactly as prescribed — do not skip doses or stop suddenly, as this can cause rebound high heart rate. Common side effects include fatigue, dizziness, and cold hands or feet.

topics.title: Statins
topics.body:
Statins are prescribed to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol and reduce the risk of future cardiac events. Research shows they also have anti-inflammatory benefits that protect the heart after surgery.

Common statins include atorvastatin (Lipitor), rosuvastatin (Crestor), and simvastatin (Zocor). Take your statin at the same time each day, usually in the evening. Report any unexplained muscle pain, weakness, or dark urine to your doctor immediately, as these may indicate a rare but serious side effect called rhabdomyolysis.

topics.title: Heart-Healthy Eating
topics.body:
A heart-healthy diet plays a critical role in your long-term recovery. Focus on: plenty of fruits, vegetables, and whole grains; lean proteins such as fish, chicken, and legumes; healthy fats from olive oil, avocados, and nuts; and low-fat dairy products.

Limit sodium to less than 2,000 mg per day to control blood pressure and reduce fluid retention. Avoid saturated fats, trans fats, processed meats, and sugary beverages. If you were prescribed a fluid restriction, follow it carefully and track daily fluid intake.

topics.title: Exercise & Activity
topics.body:
Cardiac rehabilitation is the gold standard for safely returning to physical activity after heart surgery. A supervised rehab program includes monitored exercise, education, and emotional support, typically for 12 weeks.

If not in a formal program, begin with short daily walks of 5–10 minutes and increase by 5 minutes each week as tolerated. Aim for 30 minutes of moderate-intensity activity (brisk walking) most days of the week by 8–12 weeks post-surgery. Always stop and rest if you experience chest pain, severe shortness of breath, or dizziness.

topics.title: Smoking Cessation
topics.body:
If you smoke, quitting is the single most impactful step you can take to improve your heart health and surgical outcomes. Smoking damages blood vessels, reduces oxygen in the blood, and significantly increases the risk of future heart attacks and complications.

Speak with your care team about cessation resources: prescription medications (varenicline, bupropion), nicotine replacement therapy (patches, gum, lozenges), and counseling. Every day without cigarettes reduces your cardiovascular risk.

topics.title: Call Your Doctor If...
topics.body: Contact your surgeon or cardiologist promptly if you experience: fever above 38°C (100.4°F); increased redness, swelling, or discharge from your incision; chest pain that is new or different from expected post-surgical discomfort; palpitations (rapid or irregular heartbeat); shortness of breath that is worsening; unexplained weight gain of more than 1 kg (2 lbs) overnight or 2 kg (4 lbs) in a week (may signal fluid retention); leg swelling or calf pain (possible blood clot).

topics.title: Call 911 Immediately If...
topics.body:
Go to the emergency room or call 911 immediately if you experience: crushing or severe chest pain; difficulty breathing or inability to breathe; loss of consciousness or fainting; sudden weakness or numbness in your face, arm, or leg (possible stroke); sudden severe headache with no known cause; coughing up blood; sudden vision changes.

Do not drive yourself to the hospital. Call emergency services and wait for help. Time is critical — faster treatment means better outcomes.

topics.title: Emotional Health
topics.body:
It is completely normal to feel anxious, sad, or overwhelmed after heart surgery. Studies show that up to 30–40% of cardiac surgery patients experience depression or anxiety during recovery. These feelings can slow physical healing if left unaddressed.

Talk to your care team if you feel persistently sad, have lost interest in activities, feel hopeless, or are having trouble sleeping. Cardiac rehabilitation programs include emotional support components. Support groups, counseling, and medication are all effective options. You do not have to navigate recovery alone.
